scopeNote: Buy-side research desks inside a multi-strategy hedge fund / institutional asset manager. IMPORTANT REGULATORY FRAMING: the detailed research-analyst rulebooks (FINRA 2241/2242, SEC Regulation AC, IOSCO sell-side analyst principles) bind BROKER-DEALER (sell-side) research, not buy-side desks. Buy-side research is governed indirectly: Advisers Act antifraud and compliance provisions (Sections 204A, 206, Rules 204A-1, 204-2, 206(4)-7), insider-trading law (Exchange Act 10(b)/Rule 10b-5, misappropriation theory), EU/UK MAR and research-payment rules (MiFID II, FCA COBS 2.3B / PS24/9), and voluntary standards (CFA Institute Code & Standards, Asset Manager Code; AIMA sound-practice guides). Fed/OCC/FDIC model-risk guidance (SR 11-7, superseded April 2026 by interagency SR 26-2) is bank guidance adopted by asset managers as best practice by analogy, not obligation. Each department entry distinguishes regulatory requirement vs. accepted practice vs. firm-specific judgment.

departments.name: Global Macro Research
departments.mandate: Form differentiated, falsifiable views on macroeconomic variables — growth, inflation, monetary and fiscal policy, external balances, politics/geopolitics — across G10 and EM, and translate them into tradeable theses in rates, FX, sovereign credit, equity indices and commodities. Provide the firm's top-down regime and scenario context to PMs, risk, and the other research desks.
responsibilities: Maintain country/regional economic dossiers and forecast tables (growth, inflation, policy rate paths, fiscal balances)
Cover the economic-data calendar: previews, nowcasts, real-time reaction, post-print revision of views
Cover central bank meeting cycles (Fed/ECB/BoJ/BoE and relevant EM banks): pre-meeting analysis of market-implied pricing vs. house view, post-meeting review
Produce trade theses with explicit thesis, mispricing rationale, expression, entry/exit/invalidation levels and catalysts
Run cross-market relative value, curve, and carry screening (carry-to-vol rankings, curve steepeners/flatteners, cross-sovereign spreads)
Scenario and regime analysis feeding risk management stress narratives
Monitor elections, geopolitical events, and policy processes with defined market transmission channels

workflows.name: Economic data-release cycle
workflows.cadence: Daily/weekly, keyed to the data calendar
workflows.description: Preview key releases with a house estimate and market-implied expectation; nowcast tracking; same-day reaction note assessing surprise vs. positioning; update forecast tables.
inputs: Official statistics (BLS, BEA, Eurostat, national statistics offices)

workflows.name: Central bank meeting coverage
workflows.cadence: Per meeting cycle (~8x/year per major bank) plus intermeeting speeches/minutes
workflows.description: Compare OIS/futures-implied policy path against house view; parse statements, minutes, speeches, dot plots; publish pre- and post-meeting notes with trade implications.

workflows.name: Trade thesis construction and maintenance
workflows.cadence: Continuous / ad hoc; each live thesis reviewed at least weekly and on catalyst events
workflows.description: Full thesis document: (1) falsifiable macro view, (2) why the market misprices it, (3) cleanest expression (cash, futures, swaps, options), (4) entry level, target, stop/invalidation, expected holding period, catalyst map, and risk/reward including carry and convexity. Widely accepted practice per macro practitioner literature (e.g., Graham Capital primer); exact template is a firm-level judgment call.

workflows.name: Curve, carry and relative-value screening
workflows.cadence: Daily screens; weekly published RV pack
workflows.description: Systematic screens across sovereign curves (2s10s, butterflies), cross-market spreads, FX carry-to-risk rankings, basis and inflation breakevens; anomalies routed to analysts for fundamental vetting before becoming theses.

workflows.name: Scenario and regime analysis
workflows.cadence: Quarterly, plus event-driven (elections, crises)
workflows.description: Define discrete macro scenarios with probabilities and asset-price mappings; hand scenario definitions to risk for portfolio-wide stress testing; maintain regime indicators (inflation/growth quadrants, liquidity conditions).

workflows.name: Thematic deep dives
workflows.cadence: Monthly/quarterly
workflows.description: Structural work (e.g., fiscal sustainability, demographics, energy transition, reserve currency flows) that seeds longer-horizon positioning and informs other desks' top-down assumptions.

controls: Reasonable-basis and source-documentation standard for every published view (voluntary standard: CFA Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis; firm policy makes it binding internally)

Model governance for nowcast/forecast/fair-value models: inventory, documentation, independent validation, ongoing performance monitoring — adopted by analogy to Fed/OCC/FDIC model risk guidance (SR 11-7, now SR 26-2, April 2026), which is not legally binding on fund managers

MNPI controls around political intelligence and government contacts (regulatory requirement: Advisers Act Section 204A written policies; STOCK Act makes certain government-sourced information actionable under insider-trading law)

Expert-network and consultant procedures: pre-approval, logging, chaperoning/sampling of calls, trade surveillance linkage (expected controls per SEC Division of Examinations April 2022 MNPI Risk Alert)
Books-and-records retention of research notes and recommendation records (regulatory requirement for registered advisers: Rule 204-2)
Peer challenge: pre-mortem or devil's-advocate review of major theses before capital commitment (accepted practice at centralized funds; largely absent inside multi-manager pods — firm judgment call)
departments.independence: Embedded or shared-service, never required to be independent by regulation. Two dominant patterns: (a) multi-manager platforms embed macro analysts inside PM pods with no central research authority; (b) macro-specialist and traditional firms run a central macro research team as a shared service to several books. A hybrid — central 'chief economist' function plus embedded pod analysts — is common at the largest platforms.
dependencies: Market data and curve infrastructure (front-office data team)
Quant/analytics for screens and nowcast models
Risk management for scenario calibration and sizing feedback
Sell-side macro research relationships (paid under applicable research-payment regime)

handoffs.content: Trade theses with entry/exit/invalidation, conviction level, catalyst calendar; ongoing thesis-health updates and kill signals
Scenario definitions, stress narratives, regime indicators; inbound: factor exposures and crowding metrics that constrain expression choice
Outbound: signal hypotheses and economic priors for systematic strategies; inbound: screen output, nowcast estimates, backtests of proposed rules
Top-down regime context, rate-path and FX assumptions used as common inputs in their models
Outbound: expression and timing preferences around events; inbound: liquidity conditions, flow color, financing costs

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act Section 204A + Rule 204A-1 (MNPI policies, code of ethics) — binding on registered advisers
Advisers Act Rule 204-2 (books and records), Rule 206(4)-7 (compliance program) — binding
Exchange Act 10(b)/Rule 10b-5 misappropriation theory; STOCK Act (political intelligence) — binding
SR 11-7 / SR 26-2 interagency model risk management guidance — bank guidance, adopted by analogy (accepted practice, not requirement)
CFA Institute Standards I(B), V(A), V(B); Asset Manager Code — voluntary standards widely referenced in policies
practiceNotes: Macro trade construction canon (thesis / mispricing / expression; carry, RV, basis, trend, policy pillars) is practitioner convention, not regulation; documented in manager primers such as Graham Capital's Global Macro Primer
Positioning analysis via CFTC Commitments of Traders is standard input; COT is public data published weekly by the CFTC
Whether macro research 'owns' FX is a firm structure choice — many firms treat FX as a macro sub-desk rather than pairing it with commodities (see structuralRecommendations)

departments.name: Equity Research
departments.mandate: Generate and maintain fundamental long/short views on covered companies and sectors: build and own earnings models, identify catalysts and mispricings, and deliver actionable theses (with price targets, entry/exit and risk parameters) to PMs, while monitoring live positions through earnings cycles and events.
responsibilities: Maintain coverage universes by sector/geography with tiered depth (active ideas, maintenance coverage, watchlist)
Screen for candidates (valuation, quality, revisions, event-driven setups)
Perform deep fundamental analysis: 3-statement models, unit economics, competitive dynamics, management quality, earnings-quality/footnote work
Run the earnings-season cycle: preview models vs. consensus, live coverage, post-print model updates and thesis reassessment
Conduct primary research: expert-network calls, channel checks, surveys, alternative-data analysis, management meetings/conferences
Develop short theses with explicit borrow, crowding, and squeeze-risk assessment
Maintain catalyst calendars and position-monitoring dashboards for PM books

workflows.name: Idea screening and triage
workflows.cadence: Weekly systematic screens; continuous ad hoc
workflows.description: Quantitative screens (valuation vs. history/peers, estimate revisions, factor exposures) plus event pipelines (spin-offs, management changes, guidance resets) produce a triage list; analysts do 1-2 day 'quick looks' before committing to full workups.

workflows.name: Initiation / full workup
workflows.cadence: 2-6 weeks per name; a handful per analyst per quarter
workflows.description: Full model build from filings; industry map; variant perception vs. consensus explicitly stated; valuation via multiple methods (DCF, multiples, SOTP); risk section with downside case. CFA Institute research-report guidance (business description, industry/competitive analysis, multi-method valuation, explicit risks, earnings-quality footnote review) is the widely used content benchmark.
inputs: Alternative data (card panels, web/app data, satellite)
outputs: Full earnings model under version control

workflows.name: Earnings-season cycle
workflows.cadence: Quarterly, compressed into ~6-week windows
workflows.description: Pre-print: update model, quantify buy-side vs. sell-side expectations, define what would change the thesis; print day: rapid variance analysis and PM communication; post-print: model refresh and thesis reaffirm/upgrade/kill decision.

workflows.name: Primary research program
workflows.cadence: Continuous; expert calls typically several per week per analyst
workflows.description: Expert-network consultations (pre-approved and logged per compliance policy), proprietary surveys, channel checks, and management access (conferences, NDRs). All subject to MNPI controls; in EU/UK, corporate access and research consumption must be handled under research-payment rules.

workflows.name: Alternative-data workflow
workflows.cadence: Continuous ingestion; per-name signals refreshed weekly/daily
workflows.description: KPI nowcasting from card, web, geolocation and satellite data. Requires vendor due diligence covering data provenance, MNPI risk, PII/privacy and legal collection rights before use (per SEC April 2022 MNPI Risk Alert expected controls and AIMA alt-data DDQ practice).

workflows.name: Portfolio review with PM
workflows.cadence: Weekly formal; daily informal
workflows.description: Position-by-position review: thesis health, catalyst proximity, crowding, what's changed. Analyst states whether original entry rationale and invalidation levels still hold.

controls: MNPI/insider-trading framework (regulatory requirement: Advisers Act 204A): restricted/watch lists, information barriers, wall-crossing procedures for market soundings (EU/UK: MAR Art. 11), pre-clearance for private placements

Expert-network controls (expected controls per SEC April 2022 Risk Alert): due diligence on the network, pre-approval of consultations, prohibition/extra scrutiny on public-company employees, call logging, periodic chaperoning or sampling, and surveillance linking call topics to subsequent trading

Alternative-data governance: documented vendor due diligence (provenance, MNPI, privacy/PII, web-scraping legality), contract review, periodic re-assessment (SEC Risk Alert; AIMA 'Casting the Net' DDQ practice)
Personal trading: code of ethics, access-person reporting, pre-clearance of IPOs/limited offerings (regulatory requirement: Rule 204A-1)
Evidence standard: every recommendation must have documented reasonable basis with sources retained (Rule 204-2 records; CFA Standard V(A) as the articulated bar)

Model hygiene: earnings models under version control with assumption logs; formal validation usually applied only to systematic/valuation models, not per-name spreadsheets (firm judgment call; SR 26-2 materiality-based approach is the emerging analogy)

Peer review: centralized firms use investment-committee or sector-team challenge before position initiation; multi-manager pods substitute PM accountability plus central risk limits (explicit firm-level variation)
departments.independence: Embedded. Buy-side equity analysts work for the PM/CIO who owns the capital; no regulation requires separation from portfolio management (the FINRA 2241 separation regime applies to broker-dealers, where research must be insulated from investment banking/trading pressure). The relevant buy-side independence concern is intellectual honesty against position bias — managed through culture, thesis-kill criteria, and (at some firms) devil's-advocate review, all judgment calls.
dependencies: Sell-side research and corporate access (paid via commissions/CSAs under Section 28(e) in the US; under COBS 2.3B options including PS24/9 joint payments in the UK; MiFID II regime in the EU)
Compliance (expert networks, restricted list, alt-data vendor approval)

handoffs.content: Initiation memos, earnings previews/reviews, catalyst calendars, explicit entry/exit/stop and sizing recommendations; thesis-kill notifications
Outbound: fundamental context on concentrated positions, event risk (earnings dates, binary catalysts); inbound: factor exposure, crowding and liquidity metrics that shape sizing
Outbound: labeled fundamental judgments and KPI definitions usable as features; inbound: screening output, signal decay/crowding analytics, backtest support
Outbound: expert-call requests, alt-data vendor requests, wall-cross notifications; inbound: approvals, restricted-list updates, surveillance queries
Execution timing constraints around catalysts, borrow requirements for shorts, limit levels tied to thesis entry bands

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act Section 204A, Rules 204A-1, 204-2, 206(4)-7 — binding on registered advisers
Exchange Act Section 10(b)/Rule 10b-5; EU/UK Market Abuse Regulation (incl. Art. 11 market soundings) — binding
SEC Division of Examinations Risk Alert, 'Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues' (Apr 26, 2022) — supervisory expectations, not rules, but examination benchmarks

MiFID II research unbundling (Delegated Directive Art. 13) as amended; FCA COBS 2.3B incl. PS24/9 joint-payment option (effective Aug 1, 2024) and PS25/4 extension to fund managers — binding on EU/UK managers for how research inputs are paid
Section 28(e) safe harbor (US) for paying for research with client commissions — binding conditions if relied upon

FINRA 2241 and SEC Regulation AC — bind the sell-side producers of research consumed by the desk (analyst certification, conflict disclosures); relevant to input quality assessment, not to the desk's own conduct
practiceNotes: CFA Institute 'Equity Research Report Essentials' and Standard V(A) are the de facto content/diligence benchmarks for internal research notes even though internal notes are not 'research reports' in the regulatory sense

Multi-manager platforms emphasize catalyst-centric, expectations-vs-consensus work with days-to-months horizons; single-manager funds tolerate longer-duration intrinsic-value work — same toolkit, different weighting (practice variation, not disagreement)
Position-bias control (kill criteria, forced re-underwriting after drawdown) is entirely a firm design choice; no external standard mandates it

departments.name: Fixed-Income & Credit Research
departments.mandate: Assess issuer creditworthiness and security-level value across sovereign, investment-grade, high-yield, leveraged-loan and structured-credit markets: form default/downgrade/recovery views, find relative value across curves and capital structures, and deliver credit theses with spread targets and downside protection analysis to PMs.
responsibilities: Issuer coverage and continuous surveillance (financials, guidance, rating actions, news) across the covered universe
New-issue and refinancing analysis with pricing/allocation recommendations under primary-market time pressure
Covenant and documentation analysis (indentures, credit agreements): protections, leakage risk, liability-management vulnerability
Capital-structure and cross-issuer relative value (bonds vs. loans vs. CDS; seniority arbitrage)
Downside/stress work: covenant-breach probability, refinancing walls, recovery waterfalls under liquidation/restructuring
Internal rating/tiering of the book and watchlist governance
Distressed and special-situations analysis where mandated (bankruptcy process, committee dynamics)

workflows.name: Issuer surveillance
workflows.cadence: Continuous; formal refresh each reporting period (quarterly) and on rating/news events
workflows.description: Update operating model (revenue, EBITDA, FCF), leverage/coverage ratios, liquidity runway and covenant headroom; publish surveillance note when thesis-relevant metrics move; maintain internal rating and watchlist tier.
inputs: Third-party credit research (e.g., CreditSights, Covenant Review)
outputs: Updated credit models and internal ratings

workflows.name: New-issue analysis
workflows.cadence: Deal-driven; hours-to-days turnaround
workflows.description: Rapid credit workup from the offering memorandum/prospectus: leverage pro forma, covenant package quality vs. market standard, fair-value spread vs. comparables, recommendation on participation and limit price.
outputs: New-issue memo with participate/pass and limit

workflows.name: Covenant and documentation review
workflows.cadence: Per deal at underwriting; re-reviewed on amendment, LME rumor, or distress
workflows.description: Clause-level review of indentures/credit agreements: restricted payments, liens, asset-transfer (e.g., unrestricted-subsidiary) capacity, change-of-control; quantifies value-leakage and priming risk.
outputs: Documentation risk flags attached to positions

workflows.name: Relative value and curve screening
workflows.cadence: Daily screens; weekly RV pack
workflows.description: Spread-per-turn-of-leverage, rating-bucket and sector curves, bond-vs-CDS basis, cap-structure arbitrage screens; anomalies vetted fundamentally before recommendation.
outputs: Switch/basis trade ideas with entry/exit spread levels

workflows.name: Stress and downside scenarios
workflows.cadence: Quarterly portfolio-wide; ad hoc on macro shifts
workflows.description: Recession/rates/sector-shock scenarios applied to issuer models: covenant breaches, downgrade candidates (fallen-angel risk), refinancing-wall exposure, recovery-given-default; results shared with risk for portfolio stress aggregation.

workflows.name: Distressed / restructuring situations
workflows.cadence: Ad hoc, situation-driven
workflows.description: Process analysis (bankruptcy timeline, committee dynamics, valuation fights), fulcrum-security identification, participation decisions — with heightened MNPI controls if joining creditor committees or receiving private information (wall-crossing, restricted list).

controls: Public/private information barrier — the defining control of credit research: loan-market private-side information (lender presentations, projections), creditor-committee material and market soundings must be walled; analysts electing 'private' status are restricted from advising public-side traders on that name (regulatory basis: 10b-5 misappropriation, MAR; operational design is firm judgment but universally expected)
Restricted/watch list discipline tied to wall-crossings, big-boy letters, and NDA-gated processes (accepted practice with regulatory teeth via 204A policies)

Expert-network and consultant controls identical to equity (SEC April 2022 Risk Alert expected controls); leveraged-loan expert calls flagged as high-risk in practice literature (Hedge Fund Law Report)
Internal-rating methodology consistency: documented criteria, periodic back-review of rating accuracy vs. outcomes (accepted practice; analogous to SR 26-2 ongoing-monitoring principles)
No mechanical reliance on external ratings — document independent analysis (accepted practice; echoes post-2008 supervisory direction to reduce ratings reliance)
Reasonable-basis documentation and record retention (Rule 204-2; CFA V(A))
Peer review of large/illiquid credit commitments via credit committee at centralized funds; pod-level PM accountability at multi-managers (firm variation)
departments.independence: Embedded or centralized shared service; not regulatorily separated. Credit funds and traditional FI managers most often run a centralized credit research team serving multiple PMs (closer to a bank-style research department); multi-strategy platforms embed credit analysts in pods. Note FINRA 2242's institutional-debt-research exemption applies to sell-side distribution to sophisticated institutions — it means the debt research the desk consumes carries fewer mandated protections/disclosures than retail research, raising the desk's own burden to assess input conflicts.
dependencies: Rating agency subscriptions and covenant-analysis vendors
Macro desk for rates and cycle inputs

handoffs.content: Credit theses with spread entry/exit targets, internal rating and watchlist tier changes, new-issue participate/pass calls, downgrade/default early warnings
Issuer-level stress results, recovery estimates, refinancing-wall and fallen-angel exposure lists; inbound: concentration and liquidity constraints
Outbound: wall-crossing elections, committee participation requests; inbound: restricted-list updates, trading halts on names, information-handling instructions
Outbound: internal ratings history and covenant scores as model features; inbound: RV screens, spread-curve analytics, default-model output
Outbound: limit levels and size appetite by CUSIP; inbound: axes, market depth, TRACE color feeding RV work

regulatoryBasis: Advisers Act 204A / 204A-1 / 204-2 / 206(4)-7 — binding
Exchange Act 10(b)/Rule 10b-5 (misappropriation — central to loan/distressed information handling); EU/UK MAR incl. market soundings — binding
FINRA Rule 2242 (debt research) — binds sell-side producers; its institutional-investor exemption (Rule 2242(j)) shapes the (lower) disclosure standard of research the desk consumes
Bankruptcy-process information rules (e.g., committee confidentiality, trading orders/ethical walls approved by courts) — binding when in restructurings
CFA Standards V(A)/V(B); Asset Manager Code — voluntary
practiceNotes: Credit research is downside-first by convention: covenant breach, default, recovery and liquidity questions precede upside modeling (practitioner consensus across sources reviewed)
Spread-per-turn-of-leverage and rating-bucket RV are standard idioms; exact internal rating scales (e.g., 1-10 vs. agency-mapped) are firm-specific
The private-side election in loans (take projections and go behind the wall vs. stay public-side) is a strategic firm choice with real workflow consequences — firms differ deliberately

departments.name: Commodities & FX Research
departments.mandate: Model physical supply/demand balances and term structure for energy, metals and agriculture, and fair value, carry and flows for currencies; convert inventory, positioning, weather/geopolitical and policy information into futures, options, spread and FX theses with defined entry/exit/risk; provide inflation-relevant and terms-of-trade inputs to the macro desk.
responsibilities: Build and maintain supply/demand (S&D) balance sheets per commodity with explicit revision on each official data release
Monitor and interpret inventory data (EIA weekly petroleum, USDA WASDE, exchange warehouse stocks) and physical-market signals (freight, refinery margins, crack/crush spreads)
Term-structure analysis: contango/backwardation, storage economics, roll yield, calendar-spread opportunities
Positioning analysis from CFTC Commitments of Traders and exchange open-interest data
FX fair-value and carry framework: rate differentials, PPP/BEER-style models, flow and reserve dynamics, intervention risk
Weather, geopolitical and policy monitoring (OPEC+ decisions, sanctions, export bans, tariffs) with quantified balance impact
Seasonality studies and event playbooks (e.g., hurricane season, planting/harvest, year-end liquidity)

workflows.name: Balance-sheet update cycle
workflows.cadence: Keyed to release calendar: EIA weekly; USDA WASDE, OPEC MOMR, IEA OMR monthly; ad hoc on shocks
workflows.description: Update S&D balances after each relevant release; the tradable signal is the delta — tightening vs. loosening relative to market expectation — not the absolute level; publish a same-day read with price implications.

workflows.name: Positioning analysis
workflows.cadence: Weekly (CFTC COT publication) plus daily open-interest reads
workflows.description: Track managed-money vs. commercial positioning; flag crowding and stop-run risk; combine positioning extremes with balance-sheet direction to time entries (positioning aligned with fundamentals = confirmation; opposed = contrarian setup).
outputs: Crowding alerts to PMs and risk

workflows.name: Term-structure, carry and spread monitoring
workflows.cadence: Daily
workflows.description: Monitor curve shape vs. storage economics and inventory cover; screen calendar spreads, inter-commodity spreads (cracks, crushes, spark spreads), and FX carry-to-vol rankings; route anomalies to fundamental vetting.
outputs: Spread trade candidates with entry/exit/roll plan

workflows.name: FX valuation and flow framework
workflows.cadence: Monthly model refresh; continuous event-driven
workflows.description: Maintain fair-value models (PPP/BEER-style, rate-differential regressions), balance-of-payments and reserve-flow tracking, intervention-risk assessment; align policy-path inputs with the macro desk to avoid inconsistent house views.

workflows.name: Event and weather monitoring
workflows.cadence: Continuous with defined escalation
workflows.description: Weather-model tracking for ags/natgas, OPEC+ meeting coverage, sanctions/export-policy watch; each event mapped ex ante to balance-sheet and price scenarios; escalation protocol to PMs when thresholds hit.

workflows.name: Trade thesis construction
workflows.cadence: Continuous; formal review weekly
workflows.description: Same discipline as macro: falsifiable view, why mispriced, cleanest expression (outright, calendar/inter-commodity spread, options), entry/target/stop, carry/roll cost, catalyst map; explicit attention to position-limit and deliverability constraints on expression.

controls: Anti-manipulation awareness: research and trading conduct subject to CEA Section 6(c)(1)/CFTC Rule 180.1 (fraud-based manipulation) and Rule 180.2; theses must not rely on or coordinate with attempts to move physical/derivative benchmarks (binding law)

Position-limit and large-trader awareness: federal position limits (CFTC Part 150) and exchange limits constrain expression size; research desks flag limit-sensitive expressions to compliance (limits are binding; the research-desk flagging step is accepted practice)

Information handling for physical-market intelligence: no misappropriated confidential data (e.g., non-public government reports pre-release — binding; USDA/EIA lockup rules), documented provenance for broker/physical color (accepted practice)
Alt-data licensing and provenance for satellite/AIS/weather data (contractual/legal requirement plus SEC alt-data due-diligence expectations by analogy)
Model governance for balance-sheet and fair-value models: documentation, assumption logs, back-testing forecast accuracy per release cycle (accepted practice; SR 26-2 analogy)
Reasonable-basis documentation and retention (Rule 204-2 for advisers; CFA V(A))
departments.independence: Embedded. Commodities analysts typically sit inside commodity pods or alongside macro; FX research is frequently a macro sub-function rather than paired with commodities. No regulatory independence requirement; the operative separation is between research/trading and any physical-assets or benchmark-submission activity, where conflicts and manipulation risk are managed by compliance (binding in benchmark contexts, e.g., IOSCO PRA principles context).
dependencies: Official data pipelines (EIA, USDA, IEA, OPEC, CFTC COT)
Macro desk (policy paths, USD view, inflation linkage)

handoffs.content: Commodity/FX theses with entry/exit/stop and roll plans, balance-sheet revision alerts, positioning/crowding warnings
Outbound: commodity-price and terms-of-trade inputs to inflation/growth forecasts; inbound: policy-rate paths and USD framework used in FX and dollar-denominated commodity views
Supply-shock scenario definitions, curve-shift stress inputs, concentration/deliverability flags near expiry; inbound: limit utilization and stress results
Outbound: expression proposals near position limits, new data-source requests; inbound: limit guidance, benchmark-conduct restrictions
Outbound: roll schedules, spread-leg sequencing, event-window execution constraints; inbound: physical/paper market color, basis and freight intel

regulatoryBasis: Commodity Exchange Act + CFTC Rules 180.1/180.2 (anti-manipulation, anti-fraud) — binding
CFTC Part 150 federal position limits, exchange-set limits, large-trader reporting (Form 40 / ownership & control reports) — binding on positions; research must design expressions within them
CFTC Commitments of Traders program — public data input (not an obligation)
Advisers Act 204A/204-2 etc. for the manager entity — binding
EU/UK: MAR covers commodity derivatives and related spot; REMIT for EU energy — binding in scope
CFA Standards / Asset Manager Code — voluntary
practiceNotes: The 'trade the delta, not the level' convention for balance-sheet revisions is practitioner consensus in commodity research literature

Pairing of Commodities with FX in one desk is common in bank research but less so on the buy side, where FX usually lives with macro; treat the pairing as an org-design choice (see structuralRecommendations)
Physical-market color is high-value but provenance-sensitive; firms differ on how formally they log verbal intel — a genuine judgment call with compliance trade-offs

structuralRecommendations: Keep four desks but move FX coverage into Global Macro Research and let 'Commodities Research' stand alone (or explicitly dual-report): on the buy side FX is priced off the same policy-path/flow analysis macro already owns, while commodities is physical-data-driven; the bank-style 'Commodities & FX' pairing creates a seam through the middle of the macro process

Add a shared Research Operations/Data function across all four desks: sell-side entitlement and broker-vote management, research-payment administration (Section 28(e)/CSA, FCA PS24/9 joint-payment guardrails, MiFID II), expert-network logging, alt-data vendor due-diligence workflow, and the research repository/CRM — these are identical across desks and regulator-visible, so centralizing them is both cheaper and safer

Stand up a light model-governance layer shared with Quant rather than per-desk: inventory + materiality tiering per the SR 26-2 approach (rigorous validation only for high-materiality models such as nowcasts, balance sheets, and fair-value models; lighter control for per-name spreadsheets)

Decide explicitly between pod-embedded and centralized research per strategy rather than by default: multi-manager-style embedding maximizes speed/accountability for equities and commodities pods, while credit (public/private wall management) and macro scenario work benefit from centralized shared-service teams — a hybrid is the modal large-platform answer

Create a formal cross-desk 'house view' interlock (macro policy paths, USD, inflation) consumed by equity/credit/commodity models, with documented override rights for pods — prevents silent inconsistency across desks without imposing groupthink

Route all primary research (expert networks, physical-market intel, political intelligence) through one compliance-owned intake and logging pipeline serving all four desks — the SEC's MNPI examination expectations are the same regardless of asset class
disagreementsAndVariation: Pod vs. centralized research: multi-manager platforms (Millennium/Citadel-style) embed analysts in PM pods with no central research review and enforce quality via hard drawdown limits (e.g., publicized 5%/7.5% capital-cut/termination thresholds at Millennium per practice literature); traditional and single-manager funds use centralized teams with investment committees and formal peer review. Sources describe both as successful; there is no industry consensus on which is 'sound practice' — it is a strategy-dependent design choice

Peer review/evidence standards: CFA-style diligence standards are universally cited, but the enforcement mechanism (IC memo + committee challenge vs. PM discretion) varies sharply by firm size and strategy; fast-turn pod strategies explicitly trade formal review for speed

Research payments: US (Section 28(e) soft-dollar safe harbor), UK (FCA PS24/9 joint payments with guardrails since Aug 2024, extended by PS25/4), and EU (MiFID II as amended by the Listing Act) now diverge materially; global firms disagree on whether to run one global P&L-funded model or jurisdiction-specific payment arrangements

Sell-side research rule relevance: some compliance teams treat FINRA 2241/2242 and Reg AC concepts (certification, conflict disclosure) as internal best-practice templates for buy-side notes; others deliberately do not, to avoid implying their internal notes are regulated 'research reports.' Both positions are defensible

Model-risk formality: some managers formally adopt SR 11-7/SR 26-2-style validation for research models; many treat analyst spreadsheets as judgment tools outside model governance. SR 26-2's narrower model definition and materiality tiering (April 2026) is pulling practice toward the lighter-touch position, but bank-affiliated and larger institutional managers remain stricter
Loan-market information handling: firms consciously differ on public-side vs. private-side elections in leveraged loans (access to projections vs. trading freedom); no single accepted answer exists

GIPS: sometimes proposed as a research-relevant standard, but it governs firm-level performance presentation, not research desks; including it in research-desk controls would be scope inflation (flagged here because source lists often conflate it)

sourcesConsulted.whatItSupported: FINRA Rule 2241 equity research analyst conflict-management, insulation, disclosure and personal-trading requirements (sell-side; consumed-input standard and analogy for buy-side)
FINRA Rule 2242 debt research rules and the Rule 2242(j) institutional-investor exemption shaping the disclosure standard of consumed sell-side debt research
Regulatory Notice adopting the debt research rule; tiered retail/institutional approach rationale
SEC Regulation AC analyst certification requirements for research reports and public appearances (binds covered broker-dealer persons)

SEC Division of Examinations Risk Alert (Apr 26, 2022) 'Investment Adviser MNPI Compliance Issues' — Section 204A deficiencies and expected controls for alternative data, expert networks, value-add investors, code of ethics (direct fetch returned 403; content confirmed via search excerpts and the K&L Gates summary below)

Detailed summary of the April 2022 SEC MNPI risk alert: expected controls per deficiency category (alt-data due diligence, expert-network logging/surveillance, value-add investor inventories, access-person controls)

SR 26-2 (Apr 17, 2026) interagency Revised Guidance on Model Risk Management superseding SR 11-7 and SR 21-8; narrower model definition, materiality-tiered governance, genAI/agentic AI out of scope (confirmed via search results incl. Fed page title/date and Baker Tilly/OCC Bulletin 2026-13 summaries)
OCC adoption of the revised interagency model risk management guidance (2026)
CFA Standard V(A) Diligence and Reasonable Basis — evidence standard for research recommendations; reasonable inquiry into data sources
CFA Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity — reasonable and adequate basis supported by appropriate research

CFA Institute Asset Manager Code — manager-level diligence, third-party research evaluation (assumptions, thoroughness, timeliness, objectivity of source), documentation (PDF fetch was binary; provisions confirmed via search excerpts of the Code and exposure draft)
CFA Institute equity research report content benchmark: investment summary, business/industry analysis, multi-method valuation, financial analysis, risk section, earnings-quality footnote review

IOSCO Statement of Principles for Addressing Sell-side Securities Analyst Conflicts of Interest — eliminate/manage/disclose framework (direct fetch 403; principles confirmed via search excerpts); cited as sell-side context, not buy-side obligation

FCA PS24/9 (effective Aug 1, 2024): joint-payment (CSA-like) option for research + execution with guardrails (written policy, identifiable research charge, budgets, provider range); PS25/4 extension to fund managers
FCA multi-firm review of MiFID II research unbundling implementation — buy-side research valuation, budgeting, and payment governance practice
ESMA evidence on MiFID II research unbundling effects — EU regime context for research procurement
Law-firm confirmation of PS24/9 mechanics and guardrails for UK asset managers
CFTC Commitments of Traders program: weekly positioning breakdowns by trader category based on Form 40 classifications — core commodities/FX positioning input
AIMA Guides to Sound Practices as the industry-body sound-practice corpus for hedge fund operations
AIMA 'Casting the Net' — alternative-data usage and vendor due-diligence practice (DDQ, MNPI/privacy screening, compliance interview) for hedge funds
Expert-network compliance controls in practice: pre-approval, chaperoned/recorded calls, restrictions on public-company employees
Hedge fund expert-network insider-trading risk controls, including elevated risk in leveraged-loan contexts
Manager-published primer on global macro strategy pillars (carry, RV, basis, trend, policy) and trade-construction discipline

Practice literature on multi-manager pod structure: PM-owned books, embedded analysts, catalyst-centric research, layered risk limits (lower-authority practitioner source, used for practice color only)
Practice literature on buy-side credit research: downside-first analysis, covenant/refinancing focus, credit ratio modeling, sector relative value (practitioner source, practice color only)
Commodity balance-sheet modeling workflow: update on EIA/USDA/CFTC releases, trade the revision delta, combine positioning with balance direction (practitioner source, practice color only)

confidenceNotes: High confidence on the regulatory layer (Advisers Act provisions, SEC 2022 MNPI risk alert expectations, FINRA 2241/2242 scope, Reg AC, FCA PS24/9, SR 11-7→SR 26-2 succession, CFTC COT/anti-manipulation) — all verified against primary or top-tier legal sources. Medium confidence on desk-level workflow detail: internal buy-side workflows are not publicly documented by regulators, so cadence and artifact specifics rest on practitioner literature and manager primers; these are labeled as accepted practice or judgment calls rather than requirements throughout.
